text: Okay, here we go. I had the good fortune, which is maybe apparent by the gray beard,
to be around almost 30 years ago and was invited by Tim O'Reilly to the first kind of gathering
of people where he wanted to gather momentum behind this term called open source as something
of its own meaning and definition. And the first open source conferences, I fell in love with
protocols and built Jabber XMPP back in the 90s, again, almost 30 years ago. Thank you.
And so I've seen this whole arc of open source from the very beginning and how it's changed over
the years and really what it's become with the kind of GitHub architecture where we're at today.
And we're facing something new now. And I've been telling people code is tap water. It is that
available. It is that common. Everyone's going to have access to it. So I am putting forth an idea
that I had last fall, that the future of open source is social. And of course, it's built on
Proto. Today, when you look at most open source activity, it is forks and clones. It goes through
PRs and there's issues. It all goes to upstream. There's maintainer, a group of maintainers. And
while the ESR is original cathedral and bazaar, right, there's a lot of, you know, this looks like
a cathedral again, even though there's just a lot of little cathedrals. And I believe that we can
actually build on at Proto something that is a true bazaar, where the features and capabilities that
people are developing and creating are moving between each other peer to peer. No gatekeepers,
no upstream, no waiting for releases or merging. You just have capabilities that flow. Every repo becomes
alive. Every copy of the repo becomes alive. And there's no upstream. It's all sideways.
Today, when you think about software development, people have features they want created. It often
goes to like an architect or designer or, you know, a developer who's planning those features and creates
maybe a requirements document or a scope of what they're going to implement. And then it goes into
a developer who's actually like doing the engineering and the code and they output code.
And it gets distributed using Git. So I thought, what if we've got these coding agents that make
everything different? What if when people now are using coding agents directly, they don't have to
be developers anymore. They create a new feature or capability on top of an open source project.
They can just tell the coding agent, package up and describe this feature, how you implemented it,
not the code, the description of the feature. This capability, that is what we share. That is the new
form of open source peer-to-peer sharing. And that is what I called VIT, short for vitality,
thinking that every code can be alive and be vital, every repo. And so now any open source or any open
source project with a coding agent can take a description of a capability or a feature and it can implement it
for that person. You never need to pull a release or an upstream again. It becomes yours and it lives
in your features and capabilities coming from other people.
So the things that you can share socially, you can capture up skills, right? Today, you can take
those skills and share them and just post them. And they can come from your network, the people that
you follow. And more importantly, you can take the capabilities, these structured change instructions,
these improvements, and the people that you trust can actually publish these and you can go follow
them and then you can post and share things back. The capabilities are what really grow a code base to
adapt to your, what things that you want. And both, and the skills teach agents new things and they both
flow through the same trust network. It is a human and agent collaboration process. As a human,
you're still logging into Blue Sky. You still have to vet that to make sure that this is something safe for
it to implement. And you, if you believe in it and you've used it, you can vouch for it too, right? And those
signals go into the network very similar to likes and reposts. Your agent actually does the work to skim
the network, to look at what is available and maybe look at what capabilities it thinks you might like and
present to you things that it could implement on your behalf. It can, you can tell it to remix those into your
code base. Then you can package it up and say, ship this new thing that I did back into the network.
And other agents out there using that will be able to see those capabilities. And you can tell it to
learn a new skill, like go look for other people that I'm following and skills that they've published
and go learn that skill. It's all built on AppRoto. These are just lexicons that live in your PDS
with posts. It is your trust graph. It is your agents that work the network on your behalf and
everybody can build, share, build, share, build, share. That is VIT. And it is as easy to get started
as NPM install VIT. And you can VIT login and you can go initialize any open source project to use VIT
and use your coding agent. It will know how to do everything. It comes with a skill and we can make
the world of open source social in the future. That's it. So there's a whole
